reasoning: The finding is technically accurate that cpel_flag_svg() has no internal guard on $flag_code before passing CPEL_DIR . "/assets/flags/$flag_code.svg" to file_exists(). However, the exploitability chain does not hold up under adversarial scrutiny. All internal callers (language-flag.php:65-66 and polylang-language-switcher.php:763-764) pre-filter $flag_code through cpel_flag_code(), whose regex capture group \w+ restricts the value to [a-zA-Z0-9_], which cannot contain '/' or '.' — making path traversal impossible on all real call paths. The only attack scenario is a third-party plugin explicitly calling cpel_flag_svg() with unsanitized attacker-controlled input; this requires code already executing in the PHP server context, which is a far stronger capability than the file_exists() oracle would provide. The sink itself is file_exists() only — no file content is read, no code is executed, no data is written. Even in the hypothetical abuse scenario, the attacker gains only a boolean existence check on an arbitrary path, which is not a meaningful vulnerability in a WordPress plugin context where filesystem access is routine. There is no AJAX, REST, or form endpoint in the plugin that routes external/HTTP input directly to cpel_flag_svg(). This is a missing defense-in-depth internal guard, not an exploitable vulnerability.
